questions.q: What are Azure Functions?

answer.oneLiner: Serverless compute that runs small pieces of code on demand in response to a trigger, with no server management and pay-per-execution billing.
keyPoints: Event-driven: code runs when a trigger fires
Auto-scales out based on load
Pay for execution time and resources used
Good for glue code, APIs, scheduled jobs, event processing
followUps: What triggers can start a function?
How does billing work on Consumption plan?

questions.q: What is a trigger in Azure Functions?

answer.oneLiner: A trigger is what causes a function to run — every function has exactly one.
keyPoints: HTTP, Timer (CRON), Queue, Service Bus, Blob, Event Grid, Cosmos DB
Exactly one trigger per function
Carries the data that started the execution
followUps: How is a trigger different from a binding?

questions.q: What are bindings, and how do they differ from triggers?

answer.oneLiner: Bindings are declarative connections to other services so you avoid writing boilerplate SDK code; input bindings pull data in, output bindings push data out.
keyPoints: Trigger = what starts the function (one only)
Input binding = data fetched and handed to you
Output binding = where your return value goes
Configured via attributes or function.json

code.note: Queue trigger in, blob output out — no SDK calls written.

followUps: Can you have multiple output bindings?

questions.q: Consumption plan vs Premium plan for Functions — when do you use each?

answer.oneLiner: Consumption is cheapest and scales to zero but has cold starts; Premium keeps pre-warmed instances, supports VNET, and removes cold start at higher cost.
keyPoints: Consumption: pay-per-use, scales to zero, cold starts, 5–10 min timeout
Premium: pre-warmed instances (no cold start), VNET integration, unlimited duration
Use Consumption for spiky/low-traffic; Premium for latency-sensitive or VNET needs
Dedicated/App Service plan if you already have spare capacity
followUps: How does Premium avoid cold starts?

questions.q: What are Durable Functions?

answer.oneLiner: An extension that lets you write stateful orchestrations and long-running workflows in code, with state and checkpoints managed automatically.
keyPoints: Orchestrator function coordinates activity functions
State persisted automatically (table/queue storage)
Patterns: function chaining, fan-out/fan-in, async HTTP, human interaction
Orchestrator code must be deterministic (no DateTime.Now, no random)

code.note: Orchestrator replays to rebuild state — keep it deterministic.

followUps: Why must orchestrator code be deterministic?
What is fan-out/fan-in?
redFlags: Calling DateTime.Now or HttpClient directly inside an orchestrator

questions.q: What is a cold start and how do you reduce it?

answer.oneLiner: A cold start is the latency when a new instance spins up after the function was idle/scaled to zero — the first request waits for the runtime and dependencies to load.
keyPoints: Use Premium plan (pre-warmed) or set min instances
Pick a faster runtime/smaller deployment, trim dependencies
Keep functions warm with a timer ping (Consumption workaround)
Avoid heavy static initialization in the constructor
followUps: Why doesn't Premium plan suffer cold starts?

questions.q: Stateless vs stateful functions — what's the difference?

answer.oneLiner: Normal Functions are stateless — each invocation is independent; Durable Functions add stateful orchestration where execution state survives across awaits and restarts.
keyPoints: Stateless: no memory between calls, scale freely, store state externally
Stateful (Durable): orchestration state + entities persisted
Default to stateless; reach for Durable only for workflows
followUps: What are Durable Entities?

questions.q: What are Logic Apps?

answer.oneLiner: A low-code/no-code workflow service to automate and integrate apps and data using a visual designer and hundreds of prebuilt connectors.
keyPoints: Trigger + sequence of actions = a workflow
300+ connectors (SAP, Salesforce, SQL, Office 365, etc.)
Great for integration with little code
Consumption (per-action) and Standard (single-tenant) tiers
followUps: What is a connector?

questions.q: What is a workflow in Logic Apps?

answer.oneLiner: A workflow is the trigger plus the ordered set of actions that run when it fires, defined visually or in JSON.
keyPoints: One trigger starts it (HTTP, schedule, event)
Actions run in sequence or in parallel branches
Supports conditions, loops, scopes

questions.q: What are connectors in Logic Apps?

answer.oneLiner: Connectors are prebuilt wrappers around external services and APIs that expose triggers and actions without writing integration code.
keyPoints: Standard (free tier) and Premium (enterprise systems like SAP)
Built-in run in-process; managed call out to APIs
Custom connectors for your own APIs

questions.q: How do you implement conditions and branching in a Logic App?

answer.oneLiner: Use the Condition action for if/else, Switch for multi-way branching, and Scopes to group actions and inspect their combined status.
keyPoints: Condition → true/false branches
Switch → multiple cases on a value
For each / Until for loops
Expressions use the Workflow Definition Language

questions.q: How do you handle errors and retries in Logic Apps?

answer.oneLiner: Each action has a configurable retry policy, and you use Scopes with run-after settings (run on failed/timed out) to build try/catch-style error handling.
keyPoints: Retry policy: none, fixed, or exponential with count/interval
Configure run-after to catch a prior scope's failure
Use Terminate action to fail the run cleanly
Surface failures via alerts / dead-letter pattern
followUps: What's the default retry policy?

questions.q: Logic Apps vs Azure Functions — when do you choose each?

answer.oneLiner: Logic Apps for low-code integration/orchestration across SaaS systems via connectors; Functions for custom code and compute-heavy logic.
keyPoints: Logic Apps: visual, connector-driven, integration glue
Functions: code-first, full language control, performance
Often combined: Logic App orchestrates, calls a Function for logic
Logic Apps bill per action; Functions per execution
followUps: Can a Logic App call a Function?

questions.q: How would you find which part or stored procedure is slow in a function/integration flow?

answer.oneLiner: Use Application Insights end-to-end transaction view to see dependency timings — it breaks each call (SQL, HTTP, queue) out with duration so the slow stored proc or step is visible.
keyPoints: App Insights tracks dependencies (SQL command text + duration)
End-to-end transaction / dependency map shows the bottleneck step
For SQL drill in with Query Store / execution plan / DMVs
Add custom TrackDependency / timers around suspect blocks
followUps: How does App Insights capture SQL command text?

questions.q: What is Azure Service Bus?

answer.oneLiner: A fully managed enterprise message broker for reliable, asynchronous messaging using queues and topics with at-least-once delivery.
keyPoints: Decouples producers from consumers
Queues (point-to-point) and Topics (pub/sub)
Features: sessions, dead-letter, duplicate detection, transactions
Use for ordered, transactional, mission-critical messaging
followUps: Queue vs Topic?

questions.q: Queue vs Topic in Service Bus?

answer.oneLiner: A queue delivers each message to one consumer (point-to-point); a topic broadcasts to multiple subscriptions (publish/subscribe).
keyPoints: Queue: 1 message → 1 receiver, competing consumers
Topic: 1 message → N subscriptions, each filtered
Subscriptions use rules/filters to receive a subset
Pick queue for work distribution, topic for fan-out

questions.q: Explain the publish/subscribe pattern.

answer.oneLiner: Publishers send messages to a topic without knowing the receivers; subscribers register interest and each gets its own copy.
keyPoints: Loose coupling between sender and receivers
One-to-many delivery
Easy to add new subscribers without touching publisher

questions.q: What is a dead-letter queue (DLQ)?

answer.oneLiner: A sub-queue that holds messages that can't be processed or delivered, so they don't block the main queue and can be inspected later.
keyPoints: Auto dead-letter on max delivery count exceeded or expiry
App can explicitly dead-letter a poison message
Drain/reprocess separately for diagnostics
Monitor DLQ depth with alerts
followUps: What causes a message to be auto dead-lettered?

questions.q: PeekLock vs ReceiveAndDelete?

answer.oneLiner: PeekLock locks the message while you process and you complete/abandon it (safe, at-least-once); ReceiveAndDelete removes it immediately (fast, at-most-once, risk of loss).
keyPoints: PeekLock: lock → process → Complete (or Abandon/Dead-letter)
ReceiveAndDelete: gone on receive — crash = message lost
PeekLock is the default and safe choice
Lock has a timeout; renew for long processing

questions.q: What is duplicate detection in Service Bus?

answer.oneLiner: When enabled, Service Bus discards messages with a MessageId it has already seen within a configured time window, preventing duplicates.
keyPoints: Based on MessageId set by the sender
Configurable detection time window
Helps make producers idempotent on retries
followUps: Why still make the consumer idempotent too?

questions.q: What are Service Bus sessions and how do they relate to ordering?

answer.oneLiner: Sessions group related messages by a SessionId and guarantee FIFO ordering within that session by locking it to a single consumer.
keyPoints: Same SessionId → processed in order by one receiver
Enables ordered, stateful processing per entity
Without sessions, ordering across competing consumers isn't guaranteed
followUps: How do you guarantee message ordering then?

questions.q: How do you guarantee message ordering?

answer.oneLiner: Use Service Bus sessions (partition by SessionId) so all related messages go to one consumer in FIFO order; otherwise design consumers to tolerate out-of-order delivery.
keyPoints: Sessions = ordered within a partition key
Single consumer per session prevents reordering
Alternative: sequence numbers + reorder buffer on consumer
Trade-off: ordering limits parallelism

questions.q: What is Azure Event Grid?

answer.oneLiner: A fully managed event routing service that delivers discrete events from publishers to subscribers using a pub/sub model with near-real-time push.
keyPoints: Reactive: routes lightweight event notifications
Push-based to webhooks, Functions, queues, etc.
Built-in retry + dead-letter
Great for serverless event-driven architectures
followUps: Event Grid vs Service Bus?

questions.q: What is event-driven architecture?

answer.oneLiner: A design where components communicate by producing and reacting to events rather than calling each other directly, giving loose coupling and scalability.
keyPoints: Producers emit events; consumers react independently
Loose coupling, easy to extend
Event notification vs event-carried state transfer vs event sourcing
Eventual consistency is the trade-off

questions.q: What are publishers and subscribers in an event system?

answer.oneLiner: Publishers emit events to a topic/broker; subscribers register to receive events they care about — neither knows the other directly.
keyPoints: Publisher = source of events
Subscriber = handler that reacts
Broker/topic decouples them

questions.q: Event Grid vs Service Bus — when do you use each?

answer.oneLiner: Event Grid for reactive, lightweight event notifications ("something happened"); Service Bus for reliable commands/messages with ordering, transactions, and workflows.
keyPoints: Event Grid: push, discrete events, high fan-out, cheap
Service Bus: pull, durable messages, sessions, transactions, DLQ
Event Grid = react; Service Bus = process work reliably
Event Hub if it's high-throughput streaming/telemetry
followUps: Where does Event Hub fit?

questions.q: What are webhooks and how does event routing work?

answer.oneLiner: A webhook is an HTTP callback the service POSTs an event to; Event Grid routes events to handlers by topic and subscription filters (event type, subject prefix).
keyPoints: Webhook = endpoint you expose for push delivery
Routing via subscriptions + filters
Handshake/validation step to prove ownership
Retries with back-off on failure

questions.q: Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs Azure Service Bus?

answer.oneLiner: Kafka is a high-throughput distributed log for streaming/replay; RabbitMQ is a flexible traditional broker; Service Bus is a managed enterprise broker with rich features and no ops.
keyPoints: Kafka: log-based, replay, huge throughput, consumer offsets
RabbitMQ: routing flexibility, AMQP, self/managed
Service Bus: fully managed, sessions/DLQ/transactions, Azure-native
Streaming → Kafka/Event Hub; enterprise messaging → Service Bus

questions.q: How do you guarantee a message is not lost?

answer.oneLiner: Use durable messaging with PeekLock (complete only after successful processing), persist before publishing via the Outbox pattern, and rely on retries + dead-lettering.
keyPoints: PeekLock + Complete after success (at-least-once)
Outbox pattern: write DB + message in one transaction
Retries with back-off; DLQ for poison messages
Idempotent consumers to tolerate the redelivery
followUps: What is the Outbox pattern?

questions.q: Synchronous vs asynchronous messaging?

answer.oneLiner: Sync (e.g. HTTP request/response) blocks the caller until a reply; async (queues/events) decouples sender and receiver so the sender doesn't wait.
keyPoints: Sync: simple, immediate result, tight coupling, cascading failures
Async: resilient, scalable, eventual consistency, more complex
Use async for load leveling and decoupling, sync for immediate reads

questions.q: Explain the Request-Reply pattern in messaging.

answer.oneLiner: A sender posts a message and waits for a correlated response on a reply queue, getting request/response semantics over an async transport.
keyPoints: ReplyTo address + CorrelationId tie reply to request
Adds async resilience while keeping a response
Watch for timeouts and orphaned replies

questions.q: Explain the Publish-Subscribe pattern.

answer.oneLiner: One publisher sends an event and many subscribers each receive a copy, decoupling producer from consumers.
keyPoints: One-to-many delivery
Add subscribers without changing publisher
Implemented with topics/Event Grid

questions.q: What is the Fan-out pattern?

answer.oneLiner: A single message/event is distributed to many parallel workers or handlers to spread the load or trigger multiple actions at once.
keyPoints: Fan-out/fan-in: split work, then aggregate results
Durable Functions and topics make this easy
Increases parallelism and throughput

questions.q: What is Queue-based load leveling?

answer.oneLiner: Put a queue between a bursty producer and a consumer so the consumer drains at its own pace, smoothing spikes and protecting the backend.
keyPoints: Buffers traffic bursts
Decouples request rate from processing rate
Scale consumers based on queue depth
Prevents overload / throttling of downstream

questions.q: Explain the Retry pattern.

answer.oneLiner: Automatically retry transient failures with back-off (and jitter) instead of failing immediately, since many cloud faults are temporary.
keyPoints: Only retry transient/idempotent operations
Exponential back-off + jitter to avoid thundering herd
Cap retries, then fail/dead-letter
Use Polly in .NET
redFlags: Retrying non-idempotent calls and causing duplicates

questions.q: What is the Circuit Breaker pattern?

answer.oneLiner: It stops calling a failing dependency after a threshold of failures (opens the circuit), failing fast for a cool-down period before testing recovery.
keyPoints: States: Closed → Open → Half-Open
Prevents hammering a dead service and cascading failures
Half-open lets a trial request test recovery
Combine with retry + timeout (Polly)
followUps: How is it different from retry?

questions.q: What is the Bulkhead pattern?

answer.oneLiner: Isolate resources (thread pools, connections) into partitions so a failure or overload in one area can't sink the whole application — like watertight ship compartments.
keyPoints: Limit/partition concurrency per dependency
One slow dependency can't exhaust all threads
Often paired with circuit breaker

questions.q: What is the Saga pattern?

answer.oneLiner: A way to manage a distributed transaction as a sequence of local transactions, where each step has a compensating action to undo it if a later step fails.
keyPoints: Replaces 2-phase commit across services
Orchestration (central coordinator) vs Choreography (events)
Compensating transactions roll back partial work
Eventual consistency, not ACID
followUps: Orchestration vs choreography saga?

questions.q: What is CQRS?

answer.oneLiner: Command Query Responsibility Segregation separates the write model (commands) from the read model (queries) so each can be optimized and scaled independently.
keyPoints: Separate read and write paths/models
Often paired with event sourcing
Reads can use denormalized projections
Adds complexity — use when read/write needs diverge

questions.q: How do you ensure idempotency?

answer.oneLiner: Make repeated processing of the same message have the same effect as processing it once, typically by tracking a unique message/idempotency key.
keyPoints: Store processed message IDs; skip if already seen
Use natural idempotent operations (upsert vs insert)
Idempotency key from the client/producer
Pair with duplicate detection at the broker
followUps: Where do you store the dedup keys?

questions.q: What is a correlation ID and why is it important?

answer.oneLiner: A unique ID attached to a request and propagated through every service/message so you can trace one logical operation end-to-end across a distributed system.
keyPoints: Generated at the edge, passed via headers/message properties
Ties together logs across services
Essential for distributed tracing and debugging
Foundation for request-reply correlation

questions.q: How do you handle distributed transactions across services?

answer.oneLiner: Avoid 2-phase commit; use the Saga pattern with compensating actions and the Outbox pattern to atomically commit local state and outgoing events.
keyPoints: Saga = sequence of local txns + compensations
Outbox ensures DB write and message publish are atomic
Embrace eventual consistency
Idempotency to survive retries

questions.q: How do you prevent duplicate messages?

answer.oneLiner: Combine broker-side duplicate detection (by MessageId) with idempotent consumers that track processed IDs, since at-least-once delivery means duplicates will happen.
keyPoints: Enable Service Bus duplicate detection window
Set a deterministic MessageId on the producer
Consumer dedup store / idempotent writes
Never assume exactly-once from the transport

questions.q: How do you ensure message ordering in an integration flow?

answer.oneLiner: Partition by a key (Service Bus sessions / Kafka partition key) so related messages go to one consumer in FIFO, accepting reduced parallelism.
keyPoints: Sessions/partition key = ordered per entity
Single consumer per partition
Or add sequence numbers + reorder on consumer
Trade-off: strict ordering limits scale-out

questions.q: IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS?

answer.oneLiner: They differ by how much the provider manages: IaaS gives you VMs/infra, PaaS gives you a platform to deploy code, SaaS gives you finished software.
keyPoints: IaaS: VMs, network, storage (e.g. Azure VMs)
PaaS: managed runtime, you bring code (App Service, Functions)
SaaS: ready app (Office 365)
More you move up, less you manage

questions.q: What's the difference between scalability and high availability?

answer.oneLiner: Scalability is handling more load (scale up/out); high availability is staying up despite failures via redundancy.
keyPoints: Scale up (bigger) vs scale out (more instances)
HA: redundancy across zones/regions, no single point of failure
Measured by SLA / uptime
Different goals, often designed together

questions.q: What is fault tolerance?

answer.oneLiner: The ability of a system to keep operating correctly even when components fail, through redundancy, retries, and graceful degradation.
keyPoints: Redundancy and failover
Retry/circuit breaker for transient faults
Graceful degradation over hard failure
Design assuming things will fail

questions.q: What is a managed identity and why use it?

answer.oneLiner: An Entra ID identity automatically managed by Azure that lets your service authenticate to other Azure resources without storing any credentials.
keyPoints: No secrets/connection strings in code
System-assigned (tied to resource) vs user-assigned (shared)
Used with Key Vault, SQL, Storage via RBAC
Azure rotates the credentials for you
followUps: System-assigned vs user-assigned?

questions.q: What is Azure Key Vault?

answer.oneLiner: A managed service to securely store and control access to secrets, keys, and certificates, with access governed by RBAC/policies and full audit logging.
keyPoints: Stores secrets, keys, certs
Access via managed identity + RBAC
Versioning, soft-delete, audit logs
Keeps secrets out of code/config

questions.q: What is OAuth2?

answer.oneLiner: An authorization framework that lets an app obtain limited access (an access token) to a resource on a user's behalf without handling their password.
keyPoints: About authorization (access), not authentication
Roles: resource owner, client, auth server, resource server
Auth Code flow (with PKCE) for apps, Client Credentials for service-to-service
Issues scoped, expiring access tokens
followUps: Which flow for a SPA?
What does OIDC add?

questions.q: What is a JWT?

answer.oneLiner: A JSON Web Token is a signed, self-contained token (header.payload.signature) carrying claims, so the resource can validate it without a server lookup.
keyPoints: Base64url header.payload.signature
Claims: iss, aud, exp, sub, scopes/roles
Signature verified with issuer's key (no DB call)
Don't put secrets in the payload — it's only encoded

questions.q: What is RBAC?

answer.oneLiner: Role-Based Access Control grants permissions by assigning roles to identities at a scope, so access is managed by role rather than per-user.
keyPoints: Role definition + assignment + scope
Built-in roles (Reader/Contributor/Owner) or custom
Scopes: management group → subscription → RG → resource
Least privilege

questions.q: How do you handle secrets management in a cloud app?

answer.oneLiner: Keep secrets out of code — store them in Key Vault and access them at runtime via managed identity, with rotation and audit enabled.
keyPoints: Key Vault + managed identity (no secrets in config)
Rotate regularly; use references not literals
Never commit secrets to source control
Audit and least-privilege access
redFlags: Connection strings/keys checked into the repo

questions.q: What is MSAL and how does app auth with Entra ID work?

answer.oneLiner: MSAL (Microsoft Authentication Library) handles acquiring and caching tokens from Entra ID (Azure AD); the app authenticates against Entra, gets tokens, and calls APIs with the access token.
keyPoints: App registered in Entra ID (client ID, redirect, scopes)
MSAL runs the OAuth2/OIDC flow and caches tokens
User flows interactive; service uses client credentials
Tokens validated by the API against Entra
followUps: Client credentials vs auth code flow?

questions.q: Access token vs ID token vs identity token?

answer.oneLiner: An access token authorizes API calls (for the resource); an ID token (a.k.a. identity token, from OIDC) tells the client who the user is — they are not interchangeable.
keyPoints: Access token: sent to API, defines scopes/permissions
ID token / identity token: proves authentication, consumed by the client
Don't use an ID token to call an API
Refresh token obtains new access tokens silently
redFlags: Sending the ID token to an API as authorization

questions.q: What is Docker and why use it in microservices?

answer.oneLiner: Docker packages an app and its dependencies into a portable container image that runs consistently anywhere, which is ideal for independently deployable microservices.
keyPoints: Same image runs on dev/test/prod (no 'works on my machine')
Lightweight vs VMs (shares the OS kernel)
Each microservice in its own container
Fast startup, easy scaling

questions.q: What is Kubernetes and what is a pod?

answer.oneLiner: Kubernetes is a container orchestrator that schedules, scales, and self-heals containers; a pod is the smallest deployable unit, wrapping one or more containers that share network/storage.
keyPoints: Handles scheduling, scaling, rollouts, self-healing
Pod = one+ containers sharing IP/volumes
Deployments manage pod replicas; Services expose them
Azure offering is AKS
followUps: Deployment vs Service vs Pod?

questions.q: Proxy vs reverse proxy?

answer.oneLiner: A forward proxy sits in front of clients (hides/represents them outbound); a reverse proxy sits in front of servers, accepting client requests and routing to backends.
keyPoints: Forward proxy: client-side, outbound filtering/anonymity
Reverse proxy: server-side, load balancing/TLS/routing
Reverse proxy examples: Nginx, APIM, App Gateway
Reverse proxy is the common API gateway role

questions.q: What is Azure API Management (APIM) and what do policies do?

answer.oneLiner: APIM is a managed API gateway that fronts your APIs to handle security, rate limiting, transformation, caching, versioning, and a developer portal; policies are the rules applied to requests/responses.
keyPoints: Gateway: single entry point, auth, throttling
Policies: rate-limit, cache, transform, validate JWT (inbound/outbound)
Versioning + revisions for safe API evolution
Developer portal for docs and self-service keys
followUps: How does rate limiting work in APIM?

questions.q: How does APIM handle rate limiting, caching, and versioning?

answer.oneLiner: Via policies: rate-limit/quota policies throttle callers, the cache-lookup/cache-store policies cache responses, and versions/revisions let you run multiple API versions safely.
keyPoints: rate-limit-by-key / quota throttle per subscription or key
cache-store + cache-lookup reduce backend load
Versioning by path/header/query; revisions for non-breaking changes
Policies scoped at API/operation/product level

questions.q: How would you secure APIs in Azure?

answer.oneLiner: Front APIs with APIM, require OAuth2/JWT validation and subscription keys, enforce HTTPS and rate limits, use managed identity for backend calls, and keep secrets in Key Vault.
keyPoints: validate-jwt policy + Entra ID for authn/authz
Subscription keys + IP filtering at the gateway
HTTPS only, rate limiting, input validation
Managed identity to backends; secrets in Key Vault

questions.q: How would you build a CI/CD pipeline for Azure Functions?

answer.oneLiner: Use a build stage (restore/test/package) and a release stage that deploys to a staging slot, runs smoke tests, then swaps to production — all in YAML with environment approvals.
keyPoints: Build: restore, test, publish artifact (zip)
Deploy to a staging slot, run health checks, then slot swap
Per-environment config from Key Vault / app settings
Service connection via service principal / OIDC; gated approvals

code.snippet:
- task: AzureFunctionApp@2
  inputs:
    appType: functionApp
    appName: $(funcApp)
    package: $(Pipeline.Workspace)/drop/*.zip
    deployToSlotOrASE: true
    slotName: staging
code.note: Deploy to staging slot, then swap for zero-downtime release.

followUps: Why deploy to a slot and swap?

questions.q: How do you monitor a distributed integration solution?

answer.oneLiner: Use Application Insights with correlation IDs and distributed tracing so a request can be followed across Functions, queues, and APIs, plus metrics, logs, and alerts in one place.
keyPoints: App Insights end-to-end transaction + dependency map
Propagate correlation/trace IDs across all hops
Centralize logs (Log Analytics / KQL queries)
Alerts on failures, latency, DLQ depth, queue length
followUps: How does distributed tracing correlate across a queue?
